model_narrative: We plan motor production across the four seasons of a year. In each season we decide how many motors to make, how much inventory to carry, how many employees to keep on each shift, and how many people to hire or fire, and we decide once for the whole year whether to take a lease option that covers space. Production follows a piecewise-linear curve that ties labor to motor output, and shifts can be opened or left closed. Costs cover materials, shift operation, hiring and firing, leasing, and space rental, and costs in later seasons are discounted. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole year.

sets.name: q
sets.doc: the seasons of the planning horizon in chronological order; the first member is the opening season and each later member immediately follows the one before it

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the work shifts that can be run within a season

sets.name: l
sets.doc: the breakpoints of a piecewise-linear production curve; each breakpoint pairs a labor level with the motor output it produces

params.name: d
params.doc: the motor demand to be met in each season, in motors

params.name: lc
params.doc: the leasing cost incurred in a season if the lease option is taken, in dollars

params.name: ei
params.doc: the externally given change in employment entering each season, such as a starting headcount in the opening season, in employees

params.name: delt
params.doc: the discount factor applied to costs incurred in each season, a dimensionless multiplier

params.name: mc
params.doc: the material cost per motor produced, in dollars per motor

params.name: sr
params.doc: the space rental cost charged per unit of inventory held beyond the leased allowance, in dollars per motor

params.name: hc
params.doc: the cost to hire one employee, in dollars per employee

params.name: fc
params.doc: the cost to fire one employee, in dollars per employee

params.name: invmax
params.doc: the inventory allowance covered by the lease, equal to the total demand over all seasons, in motors

params.name: pr_motor
params.doc: the piecewise-linear production curve, keyed by row then breakpoint; the 'labor' row gives the employment used at each breakpoint and the 'motor' row gives the motor output at each breakpoint. Reference a cell as model.pr_motor['labor', l] or model.pr_motor['motor', l]

params.name: sc
params.doc: the shift cost curve, keyed by row then shift; the 'fixed' row gives the fixed cost of opening a shift and the 'labor' row gives the cost per employee on that shift. Reference a cell as model.sc['fixed', s] or model.sc['labor', s]

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total discounted cost over the whole horizon, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: dpc
vars.doc: the direct production cost in each season, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: isc
vars.doc: the inventory-related cost in each season, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: wfc
vars.doc: the workforce-change cost in each season, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: src
vars.doc: the space rental cost in each season, in dollars

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the number of motors produced in each season

vars.name: ss
vars.doc: the weight placed on each production-curve breakpoint for a given season and shift; the weights select a point along the piecewise-linear curve

vars.name: ssb
vars.doc: the binary selector accompanying the piecewise-linear breakpoint weights; not referenced by any of the listed constraints

vars.name: inv
vars.doc: the inventory of motors carried at the end of each season

vars.name: lease
vars.doc: equals 1 if the lease option is taken for the horizon and 0 otherwise

vars.name: e
vars.doc: the total employment in each season, in employees

vars.name: se
vars.doc: the employment assigned to each shift within each season, in employees

vars.name: shift
vars.doc: equals 1 if the shift is opened in that season and 0 otherwise

vars.name: h
vars.doc: the number of employees hired in each season

vars.name: f
vars.doc: the number of employees fired in each season

constraints.name: acost
constraints.intent: The total cost for the whole year is the sum across the seasons of that season's production, inventory, and workforce costs taken together, with each season's combined cost scaled down by the discount factor that applies to that season. Set the total cost equal to this discounted sum over all seasons.

constraints.name: ddpc
constraints.intent: The direct production cost in a season comes from the motors made and the shifts run that season. For each season, charge the material cost for every motor produced, add the fixed cost of each shift that is opened and the per-employee cost for the people on each shift, and express the result in thousands of dollars. Set the season's direct production cost equal to this amount.

constraints.name: sbps2
constraints.intent: Production in a season is set by the points chosen along the production curve across all shifts. For each season, the motors produced equal the motor output read off the production curve at the chosen breakpoint weights, summed over every shift and every breakpoint.

constraints.name: sbses2
constraints.intent: The employment on a shift is set by the points chosen along the production curve for that shift. For each season and each shift, the employees assigned to the shift equal the labor read off the production curve at the chosen breakpoint weights, summed over the breakpoints.

constraints.name: sccs2
constraints.intent: For each season and each shift, the breakpoint weights chosen along the production curve must add up to whether that shift is opened, so the weights sum to one when the shift runs and to zero when it does not.

constraints.name: invb
constraints.intent: Inventory carries over from one season to the next. For each season, the ending inventory equals the inventory carried in from the immediately preceding season plus the motors produced that season minus the motors demanded that season. In the opening season there is no preceding season, so nothing carries in.

constraints.name: disc
constraints.intent: The inventory cost in a season combines the leasing charge and the space rental. For each season, take the leasing cost that applies if the lease is taken plus the space rental incurred that season, express it in thousands of dollars, and set the season's inventory cost equal to that amount.

constraints.name: dsrc
constraints.intent: Space rental is charged on inventory held beyond what the lease covers. For each season, the space rental cost must be at least the per-unit rental rate applied to the inventory carried that season in excess of the lease allowance, where the allowance counts only when the lease is taken.

constraints.name: dwfc
constraints.intent: The workforce cost in a season comes from hiring and firing that season. For each season, charge the hiring cost for every employee hired and the firing cost for every employee fired, express the total in thousands of dollars, and set the season's workforce cost equal to that amount.

constraints.name: ed
constraints.intent: The total employment in a season is the headcount spread across its shifts. For each season, the total employment equals the sum of the employees assigned to each shift.

constraints.name: eb1
constraints.intent: Employment changes from season to season through hiring and firing. For each season, the total employment equals the employment carried in from the immediately preceding season plus the people hired that season minus the people fired that season plus the externally given employment change entering that season. In the opening season there is no preceding season, so no employment carries in.

query: Operations is pitching a smoother staffing pattern that stands the second shift up in two separate quarters instead of cramming the whole surge into summer. We're looking at running the second shift in at least two quarters across the year. Fold that in and walk me through the resulting schedule and total cost.
expected_pyomo: model.two_second_shifts = Constraint(expr=sum(model.shift[q, 'second'] for q in model.q) >= 2)

query: We're carrying a mountain of finished motors all year just to feed the spring shipment, and the warehouse lease isn't free. Add up the stock we're holding at the close of every quarter and it's a huge number. Shouldn't that total carried inventory across the four quarters stay under 30000 motors? What's keeping the model from holding less overall?
expected_pyomo: model.total_carry_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.inv[q] for q in model.q) <= 30000.0)

query: The plan runs fall and winter at the exact same flat output, but winter sits right up against the spring shipment so carrying that stock is cheaper and the labor is already on the books. Shouldn't winter lean in and out-produce fall by a clear margin — say at least a thousand motors more — instead of mirroring it? What's the model seeing that keeps the two quarters locked together?
